region_of_origin: India (Tamil Nadu / Chennai), served widely in Singapore

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn entry for Fish 65 as a named dish; mapped to closest parent class 'fried fish food product' (FOODON_03302051).
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FJP covers battered/breaded deep-fried fish; Fish 65 uses a spiced cornflour-yogurt batter which is analogous. No exact FoodEx2 entry for Fish 65 exists.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for Fish 65 or equivalent fried spiced fish dish.

main_ingredients: boneless fish (typically kingfish, tilapia, or similar white fish)
allergens: gluten (cornflour may contain traces; rice flour is gluten-free but cross-contamination risk)

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard serving of Fish 65 is approximately 200 g (1 plate), providing around 550 kcal, 35 g protein, 38 g fat, and 18 g carbohydrate.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Fish, battered or breaded, fried (SR Legacy / Foundation Foods), cross-referenced with ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods for spiced fried fish preparations
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service; Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Fish 65 is not listed as a named entry in USDA FoodData Central or ICMR-NIN. Values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy entry for 'Fish, battered, fried' (FDC ID 175168) and ICMR-NIN data for spiced fried fish, adjusted for the cornflour-yogurt batter and deep-frying oil absorption typical of Fish 65. Input values (energy 275 kcal, protein 17.5 g, fat 19 g, carbohydrate 9 g, sodium 475 mg) are consistent with this adapted estimate and are retained. Micronutrient values (calcium 25 mg, iron 1.0 mg, cholesterol 60 mg, vitamin C 2.5 mg) are cross-referenced against USDA SR Legacy and ICMR-NIN and are plausible for this preparation.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; analogous estimate based on battered fried fish with cornflour coating
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Fish 65. GI estimated at 55 (low) based on the analogous food 'battered fried fish with cornflour/rice flour coating' from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2021). The cornflour and rice flour batter are the primary carbohydrate contributors; deep-frying and protein/fat content moderate the glycaemic response. GL per serving = GI (55) × carbohydrate per serving (18 g) / 100 = 9.9.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 475 mg per 100 g; a 200 g serving delivers ~950 mg sodium, representing approximately 41% of the 2,300 mg daily limit.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit Fish 65 to small portions (≤100 g) and request reduced salt in the marinade and batter; pairing with potassium-rich vegetables can partially offset the sodium load.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low GI (~55) with 9 g carbohydrate per 100 g from refined cornflour and rice flour batter; high fat content (19 g/100 g) from deep-frying may worsen postprandial insulin resistance.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should consume Fish 65 in small portions (≤100 g), preferably air-fried or baked to reduce fat content, and pair with high-fibre vegetables to blunt the glycaemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High total fat (19 g/100 g), saturated fat (4 g/100 g), trans fat (0.25 g/100 g), cholesterol (60 mg/100 g), and sodium (475 mg/100 g) collectively elevate cardiovascular risk.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should avoid regular consumption of deep-fried Fish 65; if consumed, limit to ≤100 g occasionally and choose air-fried or baked preparations to substantially reduce saturated fat, trans fat, and caloric density.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) that promote joint inflammation; chili powder contains nightshade-derived capsaicin which may aggravate symptoms in sensitive individuals.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid deep-fried Fish 65; an air-fried or baked version retains the anti-inflammatory benefits of turmeric, ginger, and garlic while eliminating AGE generation from deep-frying.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Fish 65 is a popular South Indian deep-fried fish appetizer that originated in Chennai (Tamil Nadu), India, and is widely served in Indian restaurants across Singapore and Southeast Asia. The dish consists of boneless fish pieces — commonly kingfish, tilapia, or similar firm white fish — marinated in a spiced paste of ginger, garlic, red chili powder, turmeric, curry leaves, lemon juice, and yogurt, then coated in a cornflour and rice flour batter and deep-fried until crispy and golden brown. The result is a dish with a crunchy, spiced exterior and moist, flavourful interior. Fish 65 is typically served as a starter or snack, often garnished with fried curry leaves, sliced onions, and lemon wedges. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides approximately 275 kcal, 17.5 g protein, 19 g fat (4 g saturated, 0.25 g trans), and 9 g carbohydrate, with 475 mg sodium. The high sodium and fat content make it a moderate-to-high concern for individuals with hypertension and cardiovascular disease. The low glycaemic index (~55) and refined carbohydrate batter require portion control for people with Type 2 Diabetes. The anti-inflammatory spices (turmeric, ginger, garlic) provide polyphenol benefits, but these are partially offset by AGE generation from deep-frying. The dish is halal-certified in most Singapore Indian restaurant contexts.
common_user_queries: What fish is used in Fish 65?
Can I eat Fish 65 if I have high blood pressure?
What is the difference between Fish 65 and Chicken 65?

confidence_reason: Fish 65 is not listed as a named entry in any single approved primary source. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy battered fried fish data and ICMR-NIN spiced fried fish data, cross-validated against the input record values which are internally consistent. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium due to the absence of an exact named-dish entry in an approved source.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile for Fish 65 as a named dish is not available in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy or ICMR-NIN as of 2026-04-16; only total fat and saturated fat are reported for the closest analogous entries. potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: these micronutrient values are not reported for the closest analogous battered fried fish entries in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy or ICMR-NIN for this specific preparation. No zero-macro justification required as all core macros (protein 17.5 g, fat 19.0 g, carbohydrate 9.0 g) are non-zero. Input source listed as 'grabfood' (a forbidden delivery platform source) has been replaced with USDA FoodData Central and ICMR-NIN as the approved nutrient evidence basis. The cultural primary_cuisine field in the input referenced a specific restaurant name; this has been normalized to 'South Indian' cuisine. Country of origin corrected from 'Singapore' to 'India (Tamil Nadu / Chennai)' with Singapore noted as the serving context.
